posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Mom is making escargot tonight. Im hunkered down with most wine moved out of the house in prep for Irma, so my options are:

2015 Rieslings (Prum, Donnhoff, Willi)
2012 White Bordeaux (Pape Clemente)
2010 Sauternes
1995 Kalin Chardonnay

Didn’t think ahead for this one, but didn’t know my mother was bringing snails, either.

posts.name: Stephen Pepe
posts.cooked: White Bordeaux. Stay safe.

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked: Base the pairing on the sauce. I would pair Joel Antunes’ magnificent escargot in a fresh corn broth very differently than escargot in garlic butter.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Garlic butter, going to traditional Cooking route.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: Drink it all.

posts.name: Nowell Karten
posts.cooked: As a lover and drinker of German Riesling, I’ve never thought prominent garlic to be a great pairing.

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked:
I wouldn’t hesitate to go with a more rustic style Loire Cab Franc like a Raffault Chinon Picasses and let the earthy notes in the wine play off the earthy snails. A Beaujolais with its brightness could also work well for its contrast.

If the whites you mention, I’d go with the Kalin. Escargot is a traditional Burgundian dish, so Chardonnay is appropriate and older Kalins tend to blend the vibrancy and richness I would want to go with a rich garlic butter. Maybe think about serving the snails with fresh lemon wedges to allow reach person to experiment with adjusting the acidity of the food to that of the wine.

Nowell Karten:

As a lover and drinker of German Riesling, I’ve never thought prominent garlic to be a great pairing.

Agreed, I feel like it makes the garlic taste very bitter; especially the 2015s. I would second the Burg.

Stay safe!

posts.name: Rob Isaacs
posts.cooked: +1 Kalin

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:
Mike Evans:

I wouldn’t hesitate to go with a more rustic style Loire Cab Franc like a Raffault Chinon Picasses and let the earthy notes in the wine play off the earthy snails. A Beaujolais with its brightness could also work well for its contrast.

If the whites you mention, I’d go with the Kalin. Escargot is a traditional Burgundian dish, so Chardonnay is appropriate and older Kalins tend to blend the vibrancy and richness I would want to go with a rich garlic butter. Maybe think about serving the snails with fresh lemon wedges to allow reach person to experiment with adjusting the acidity of the food to that of the wine.

Great call!

posts.cooked:

Mike Evans:

I wouldn’t hesitate to go with a more rustic style Loire Cab Franc like a Raffault Chinon Picasses and let the earthy notes in the wine play off the earthy snails. A Beaujolais with its brightness could also work well for its contrast.

If the whites you mention, I’d go with the Kalin. Interesting. Would not have thought reds. I do have a 1990 Raffault and a 2993 Breton. I’m doing it!

posts.name: NED VALOIS
posts.cooked: Order me one of those 2993 Bretons !

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

Interesting. Would not have thought reds. I do have a 1990 Raffault and a 2993 Breton. I’m doing it!

I hope you didn’t buy those futures from Premier Cru.

posts.name: Diane Kessler
posts.cooked: I wouldn’t consider any pairing other than Chardonnay and escargot.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Lol! 1993.

And for the record, I had 100% success with those PC bad folk.

posts.name: Andrew Taylor
posts.cooked:
The classics would be with Chablis first, or a Champagne (extra brut), Quincy or Reuilly.

Not to be a total food bitch, but never seen anyone in France putting a red on traditional escargots.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
ATaylor:

The classics would be with Chablis first, or a Champagne (extra brut), Quincy or Reuilly.

Try telling that to the folks eating escargots in Meursault…

The Kalin Chardonnay would be my choice.

posts.name: Ilkka |_ |_| mme
posts.cooked: I do not think it is at all about the snails but instead what else there is on the plate. If it is something really fatty (as it tends to be) then it is best to go with something that cleans the palate effectively like Champagne or Cremant de Bourgogne. I do not know the Chardonnay but if the acidity is there I guess that would be my choice. If not then Riesling.

posts.name: Mike Francisco
posts.cooked:
Diane Kessler:

I wouldn’t consider any pairing other than Chardonnay and escargot.

+1

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Lol, with all the wind-up, we forgot the dang snails.

But, I did pop a killer 1993 Breton Bourgueil Les Perrieres. Textbook Loire Cab Franc. All crispy red fruits, tobacco ash, dry forest floor and bell pepper. Showing an excellent juxtaposition of ripe but tart fruits. Top-tier wine.

fancy_title: Pairing Option with Escargot  [TN: 1993 Breton Bourgueil Les Perrieres]
title: Pairing Option with Escargot  [TN: 1993 Breton Bourgueil Les Perrieres]
